scryptogram.solution: for god hath not given us the spirit of fear; but of power, and of love, and of a sound mind.

prompt.referenceText: Be strong and of a good courage: for unto this people shalt thou divide for an inheritance the land, which I sware unto their fathers to give them. Only be thou strong and very courageous, that thou mayest observe to do according to all the law, which Moses my servant commanded thee.
prompt.question: The Lord ties courage to inheritance—Joshua needs to be brave because there's a promised land waiting. What promised blessing is on the other side of an act of courage you've been avoiding?
prompt.response: Notice that the courage isn't asked for its own sake. It's the doorway to inheritance. Most of the blessings God has set aside for us require us to walk forward into something that scares us first. The fear is real; it's just smaller than what He's holding for you on the other side.

scryptogram.solution: now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen.

prompt.referenceText: And it shall come to pass, as soon as the soles of the feet of the priests that bear the ark of the Lord, the Lord of all the earth, shall rest in the waters of Jordan, that the waters of Jordan shall be cut off from the waters that come down from above; and they shall stand upon an heap... And as they that bare the ark were come unto Jordan, and the feet of the priests that bare the ark were dipped in the brim of the water, (for Jordan overfloweth all his banks all the time of harvest,) That the waters which came down from above stood and rose up upon an heap...
prompt.question: The Jordan didn't part until the priests had already stepped into the water. Where in your life is God waiting for you to put your feet in before He moves? What blessing have you been waiting for, when He's actually been waiting for you?
prompt.response: We tend to want the miracle first and the obedience second. But the order in scripture is consistent: step into the river, and then watch it part. Faith isn't certainty before the move—it's the willingness to get your feet wet on the strength of His word, and let Him show up in the act of your obeying.

scryptogram.solution: (for we walk by faith, not by sight:)

prompt.referenceText: And the Lord said unto Joshua, See, I have given into thine hand Jericho, and the king thereof, and the mighty men of valour. And ye shall compass the city, all ye men of war, and go round about the city once. Thus shalt thou do six days. And seven priests shall bear before the ark seven trumpets of rams' horns: and the seventh day ye shall compass the city seven times, and the priests shall blow with the trumpets. And it shall come to pass, that when they make a long blast with the ram's horn, and when ye hear the sound of the trumpet, all the people shall shout with a long blast: and the priests shall blow the trumpets, and the priests shall shout with a great shout; and the wall of the city shall fall down flat.
prompt.question: Marching silently around a city for six days looked ridiculous. The courage Israel needed wasn't to fight—it was to obey instructions that didn't seem to do anything. Where is God asking you to do something that feels too small or too strange to matter?
prompt.response: Sometimes the courage to reach for a blessing is the courage to look foolish doing it. Showing up to scripture study when you don't feel anything, praying when the answer hasn't come, keeping a covenant when no one would notice if you didn't—these are the silent laps around your Jericho. The walls don't fall on day one. They fall when you've been faithful long enough that the seventh day arrives.

scryptogram.solution: even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead, being alone.

prompt.referenceText: And she said unto the men, I know that the Lord hath given you the land, and that your terror is fallen upon us, and that all the inhabitants of the land faint because of you. For we have heard how the Lord dried up the water of the Red sea for you, when ye came out of Egypt; and what ye did unto the two kings of the Amorites, that were on the other side Jordan, Sihon and Og, whom ye utterly destroyed. And as soon as we had heard these things, our hearts did melt, neither did there remain any more courage in any man, because of you: for the Lord your God, he is God in heaven above, and in earth beneath.
prompt.question: Rahab heard the same stories as everyone else in Jericho, but she alone acted on what she heard. What truth have you already been told about God that you haven't yet bet your life on?
prompt.response: Rahab is one of scripture's most surprising heroes—an outsider with little to gain and everything to lose. Her courage wasn't louder faith; it was faith that moved her hands. She hid the spies, hung the scarlet cord, and reached for a blessing she had no business inheriting. God always seems to honor that kind of reach.

scryptogram.solution: but his delight is in the law of the lord; and in his law doth he meditate day and night. and he shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water, that bringeth forth his fruit in his season.

prompt.referenceText: This book of the law shall not depart out of thy mouth; but thou shalt meditate therein day and night, that thou mayest observe to do according to all that is written therein: for then thou shalt make thy way prosperous, and then thou shalt have good success.
prompt.question: On the eve of the biggest assignment of his life, the Lord tells Joshua not to train, not to strategize, but to soak in the scriptures day and night. Why is that the right preparation for hard things—and what would change if you treated your scripture time as actual battle prep?
prompt.response: Joshua needed courage for war, and God gave him a reading list. That's because the kind of courage that takes promised lands isn't manufactured in the moment—it's grown over time, in the quiet, by hearing God's voice over and over until it's louder than the giants. If your courage feels thin, the question isn't whether you're brave enough; it's whether you've been listening long enough.

scryptogram.solution: come with full purpose of heart, and cleave unto god as he cleaveth unto you. and while his arm of mercy is extended towards you in the light of the day, harden not your hearts.

prompt.referenceText: But cleave unto the Lord your God, as ye have done unto this day.
prompt.question: Cleaving means refusing to let go—even when it costs. What are you holding so tightly that it's competing with the Lord for the same hand? What would it look like to cleave to Him with the grip you're currently using on something else?
prompt.response: Cleaving is a quiet kind of courage. It doesn't make headlines, but it survives every storm that does. Joshua's final counsel after a lifetime of conquest wasn't "do more"—it was "don't let go." The blessings of the covenant aren't seized in a single bold moment so much as held onto, day after day, until the end.

scryptogram.solution: have ye spiritually been born of god? have ye received his image in your countenances? have ye experienced this mighty change in your hearts?

prompt.referenceText: Now therefore fear the Lord, and serve him in sincerity and in truth: and put away the gods which your fathers served on the other side of the flood, and in Egypt; and serve ye the Lord. And if it seem evil unto you to serve the Lord, choose you this day whom ye will serve; whether the gods which your fathers served that were on the other side of the flood, or the gods of the Amorites, in whose land ye dwell: but as for me and my house, we will serve the Lord.
prompt.question: Joshua doesn't ask Israel to choose well—he asks them to choose, period. The little gods of Egypt were still in their tents after forty years in the wilderness. What god has quietly traveled with you that it's time to put away? What would 'as for me and my house' actually require of you this week?
prompt.response: The bravest thing Joshua does in this whole book isn't crossing the Jordan or taking Jericho—it's standing in front of his entire nation and saying, out loud, what his house is going to do. Reaching for the biggest blessings always involves declaring something. A choice not declared usually isn't a choice yet.
